content.problemStatement:
Chef defines a pair of positive integers (a,b)(a, b)(a,b) to be a Oneful Pair\text{Oneful Pair}Oneful Pair, if

a+b+(aâ‹…b)=111a + b + (a \cdot b) = 111a+b+(aâ‹…b)=111

For example, (1,55)(1, 55)(1,55) is a Oneful Pair\text{Oneful Pair}Oneful Pair, since 1+55+(1â‹…55)=56+55=1111 + 55 + (1 \cdot 55) = 56 + 55 = 1111+55+(1â‹…55)=56+55=111.

But (1,56)(1, 56)(1,56) is not a Oneful Pair\text{Oneful Pair}Oneful Pair, since 1+56+(1â‹…56)=57+56=113â‰ 1111 + 56 + (1 \cdot 56) = 57 + 56 = 113 \neq 1111+56+(1â‹…56)=57+56=113î€ =111.

Given two positive integers aaa and bbb, output `Yes` if they are a Oneful Pair\text{Oneful Pair}Oneful Pair. And `No` otherwise.


Input Format
The only line of input contains two space-separated integers aaa and bbb.



Output Format
Output `Yes`, if (a,b)(a, b)(a,b) form a Oneful Pair\text{Oneful Pair}Oneful Pair. Output `No` if they do not.


You may print each character of `Yes` and `No` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
